data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-06T01:38:28.108Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating a .NET 10 web API to .NET 11. The app uses EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider.
We have a repository pattern where some methods use synchronous LINQ:

```csharp
public class ProductRepository
{
    private readonly CosmosDbContext _context;

    public List<Product> GetAll()
    {
        return _context.Products.ToList();
    }

    public Product GetById(int id)
    {
        return _context.Products.First(p => p.Id == id);
    }

    public int GetCount()
    {
        return _context.Products.Count();
    }

    public void Save(Product product)
    {
        _context.Products.Add(product);
        _context.SaveChanges();
    }
}
```

In .NET 10 we had the sync-over-async opt-in configured. What breaks in .NET 11?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about what breaks when migrating from .NET 10 to .NET 11 with EF Core using the Azure Cosmos DB provider, specifically around synchronous LINQ operations.

This is about a known breaking change in EF Core. Let me explain what I know:

In EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider, synchronous operations were never natively supported — they were implemented as sync-over-async wrappers. In EF Core 10 (.NET 10), there was an opt-in mechanism to allow this pattern. In .NET 11 (EF Core 11), this sync-over-async support is being removed entirely.


I'm trying to pin down the timeline here. The user mentioned May 2026 as the current date, which would put .NET 10's release in November 2026 and .NET 11 in November 2027, so this is a hypothetical scenario. What I'm confident about is that the Cosmos DB provider in EF Core has always had limitations with synchronous operations — it's fundamentally async-only by design, and any sync wrappers were workarounds rather than proper support.

The core issue is that synchronous LINQ operators like `ToList()`, `First()`, `Count()`, and `SaveChanges()` were implemented using sync-over-async patterns internally, which blocks threads and can cause deadlocks. EF Core has been moving away from this, and in EF Core 9, the Cosmos provider started throwing exceptions for sync operations by default, though there was an opt-in flag to temporarily re-enable them. The breaking change the user is describing seems to be about whether that opt-in still exists in .NET 10's version of EF Core. `SaveChanges()` also needs to become `await SaveChangesAsync()`, though local operations like `Add()` don't require changes. The solution is straightforward: convert all synchronous LINQ and DbContext operations to their async equivalents throughout the codebase.
